name: org.machanism.machai:ghostwriter
description: Ghostwriter is a Java 8 Maven artifact and command-line documentation engine for repository-aware AI-assisted software engineering workflows. The generated API documentation for Ghostwriter 1.2.0-SNAPSHOT describes a filesystem-oriented tool that scans source code, documentation, project site content, and other repository files for embedded @guidance directives, gathers project layout metadata, builds prompts, and executes those prompts through a configured org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider. It supports two major modes: guidance-driven processing through org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.GuidanceProcessor, and reusable TOML Act workflows through org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor. The processor package contains the CLI entry point org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.Ghostwriter, traversal and filtering support in AbstractFileProcessor, AI request assembly and tool integration in AIFileProcessor, episode sequencing through Episodes, configuration constants in GWConstants, and runtime exceptions for missing acts or episodes. The reviewer package defines org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer as the SPI for inspecting project artifacts and includes Java, Markdown, HTML/XML, TypeScript, Python, PlantUML, and generic text guidance reviewers. The tools package exposes host-integrated function tools for project-relative file access, unified diff patching, directory discovery, validated command execution with deny-list checks and bounded logs, HTTP and REST access, guidance-tag discovery, Act management, episode control, task termination, and project-scoped context variables. Ghostwriter is useful as a CLI application or embeddable library for documentation generation, guided repository maintenance, structured prompt orchestration, AI-assisted review pipelines, project automation, and host applications that need controlled local and remote tooling around repository content.

usageContext: Run java -jar gw.jar <paths> [options] from a repository to scan source, test, documentation, site, or matcher-selected files for embedded @guidance directives
Embed org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.GuidanceProcessor to extract guidance fragments from project files and execute them through a configured Genai provider
Embed org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor to execute reusable TOML-defined Act templates and ordered episodes for repeated maintenance or documentation tasks
Implement org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer to support additional file formats beyond Java, Markdown, HTML/XML, TypeScript, Python, PlantUML, and generic guidance text files

Expose org.machanism.machai.gw.tools function tools to AI workflows that need controlled project-relative file operations, shell commands, web retrieval, REST calls, Act control, and context variables
Build repository governance, documentation refresh, guided refactoring assistance, and content-generation workflows where instructions live alongside the files they govern

features.name: Ghostwriter command-line startup
features.signature: public static void main(java.lang.String[] args) throws java.io.IOException, org.apache.commons.cli.ParseException
features.description: Starts the Ghostwriter command-line application. The CLI selects scan targets, traverses the detected project layout, delegates file inspection to reviewers, composes prompts from project metadata and guidance or Act templates, registers optional function tools, and executes requests through the configured GenAI provider.

examples.description: Programmatic equivalent of starting the Ghostwriter CLI for a repository-relative scan path. Normal users typically invoke the packaged application with java -jar gw.jar src/main.

features.name: Guidance processing
features.description: Scans a project tree, uses format-specific org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer implementations to extract embedded @guidance directives, prepares repository-aware prompts, and dispatches the resulting requests to a configured Genai provider.

examples.description: Creates a guidance processor rooted at the current project. The processor can be used by host tooling that wants guidance-driven scans rather than a named Act workflow.

features.name: Act workflow processing
features.description: Runs Ghostwriter in Act mode, resolving reusable TOML-defined Acts and episodes for structured prompt workflows. Act processing supports repeated maintenance, review, documentation generation, episode flow control, custom act catalogs, and prompt execution against repository content.

examples.description: Configures an Act processor for the current project, points it at a team act directory, and selects a concrete task request.

features.name: Reviewer SPI
features.signature: java.lang.String perform(java.io.File projectDir, java.io.File file) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: Reviews a project file and returns a normalized prompt fragment when actionable @guidance instructions are present. Built-in reviewer implementations support Java, Markdown, HTML/XML, TypeScript, Python, PlantUML, and generic guidance text files.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer reviewer = new org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.MarkdownReviewer();
java.lang.String promptFragment = reviewer.perform(new java.io.File("."), new java.io.File("README.md"));
examples.description: Directly uses a Markdown reviewer to inspect a documentation file for guidance before passing the resulting prompt fragment to a larger workflow.

features.name: Host-integrated function tools
features.description: Provides optional tools that can be exposed to AI prompt execution for controlled interaction with the local and remote environment. Capabilities include reading and writing project files, applying unified diff patches, listing folders, executing validated commands with log access, fetching web pages, invoking REST APIs, discovering files with guidance tags, controlling Act episodes, ending or repeating tasks, and managing project-scoped context variables.

examples.description: Instantiates tool registrars that a host application can attach to a Genai provider for controlled file, command, and web access during Ghostwriter workflows.

customizations.name: Reviewer
customizations.description: Primary service-provider extension point for adding new artifact formats that may contain embedded @guidance instructions. Implementations declare supported file extensions and inspect matching project files to produce prompt fragments for downstream Ghostwriter processing.

examples.description: Adds a custom reviewer for JSON files. A real implementation would inspect the file and return a guidance prompt fragment when repository-specific instructions are found.

customizations.name: Custom Act catalog
customizations.description: ActProcessor can be configured to use external directories of TOML Act definitions, enabling teams to maintain organization-specific workflow templates separately from built-in acts.

examples.description: Selects a project-local acts directory before choosing and executing an Act workflow.

customizations.name: Function tool registration
customizations.description: Host applications can choose which Ghostwriter tools are available to AI execution, allowing controlled exposure of local file operations, command execution, web retrieval, REST invocation, Act lifecycle functions, and context-variable management.

examples.description: Creates optional tool registrars that can be attached to the host Genai integration when project context and Act execution capabilities are desired.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer
studs.description: Service-provider interface for components that scan project files and extract @guidance instructions for downstream processing. This is the main extension contract for adding new reviewable artifact types to Ghostwriter.

examples.description: Discovers available reviewer implementations and inspects supported file extensions before starting a repository scan.

examples.title: Install Ghostwriter as a Maven dependency and run an Act workflow
examples.scenario: Embed Ghostwriter in a Java application that executes reusable repository automation workflows.
steps: 1. Add org.machanism.machai:ghostwriter:1.2.0-SNAPSHOT to the Maven project that will host the automation.
2. Ensure Java 8 or newer is available because the artifact is compiled with maven.compiler.release 8.
3. Configure the Genai provider expected by your environment and choose a provider key such as OpenAI:gpt-5.1.
4. Create org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor with the repository root as the project directory.
5. Optionally call setActsLocation with a directory such as ./acts to load custom TOML Act templates.
6. Call setAct with a workflow name and practical request, then allow the surrounding host workflow to execute the selected episodes.
examples.description: A practical library-embedding scenario: install the Maven dependency, configure an optional Act directory, select an Act task, and run repository-aware prompt automation.

examples.title: Run the Ghostwriter CLI from the packaged jar
examples.scenario: Use Ghostwriter as a ready-to-run command-line application for guided documentation or maintenance updates.
steps: 1. Build or obtain the Ghostwriter distribution jar. The Maven pack profile creates a gw.jar artifact with org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.Ghostwriter as the main class.
2. Install Java and make sure the java command is available on PATH.
3. Open a terminal in the repository that should be scanned.
4. Configure any Genai provider credentials required by your environment.
5. Run java -jar gw.jar <paths> [options] with repository-relative paths such as src/main or matcher expressions supported by java.nio.file.FileSystems#getPathMatcher(String).
6. Review generated documentation updates, modified files, and logs after the command completes.
examples.description: Step-by-step CLI usage based on the Javadoc usage notes. Ghostwriter scans matching targets, extracts embedded guidance, and executes the configured AI workflow.

examples.title: Extend Ghostwriter with a custom Reviewer implementation
examples.scenario: Support a new project artifact type so embedded guidance in that file format can participate in Ghostwriter processing.
steps: 1. Create a class that implements org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer.
2. Implement getSupportedFileExtensions to return the file suffixes routed to the reviewer.
3. Implement perform(java.io.File projectDir, java.io.File file) to inspect a matching file and return a prompt fragment only when actionable guidance is present.
4. Register the implementation using Java ServiceLoader metadata in your build or distribution.
5. Add the extension jar to the runtime classpath used by Ghostwriter.
6. Run Ghostwriter against a directory containing the new file type and verify that the custom reviewer contributes prompt input.
examples.description: A practical extension scenario using the Reviewer SPI to add support for guidance extraction from a custom file format.

examples.title: Register Ghostwriter tools in a host-integrated AI workflow
examples.scenario: Allow a host application to provide controlled file, command, web, Act, and project-context capabilities during AI prompt execution.
steps: 1. Add the Ghostwriter dependency to the host application and initialize the Genai provider used by your automation runtime.
2. Decide which capabilities should be exposed to workflows: file operations, command execution, web and REST access, Act management, guidance processing, or project context variables.
3. Instantiate the corresponding tool registrar classes from org.machanism.machai.gw.tools.
4. Register the selected tools with the provider or execution environment according to your host integration.
5. Run a workflow that reads or patches project files, retrieves remote documentation, executes validated diagnostics, moves between Act episodes, or stores project-scoped context values.
6. Inspect bounded command logs and generated repository changes to confirm the automation result.
examples.description: A host-embedding scenario showing how Ghostwriter tools can be made available to AI-driven repository workflows while keeping access mediated through tool classes.
